summary: Muratori draws an extended analogy between modern printing presses and GPUs, inspired by his prepress work on the book Meow the Infinite. He maps GPU concepts like frame rates, multi-channel rendering, texture caches, pipeline stalls, and debug instrumentation onto their printing press equivalents. The post is deliberately impractical — a self-aware Buzzfeed-style listicle meant purely to convey enthusiasm for how impressive industrial printing technology is. He notes that web presses have fixed 'frame rates' measured in feet per second, use plate-based 'texture caches' that cause pipeline stalls on swap, and employ high-speed cameras as debug instrumentation.
key_points: Web presses operate on continuous paper feeds at fixed rates, analogous to fixed frame rate rendering, while sheet-fed presses allow variable processing per sheet
Printing presses use multi-channel rendering with four or more inking assemblies in series, and sheet-fed presses can even do multi-pass rendering by re-feeding sheets
Physical plates act as a texture cache — swapping them causes a full pipeline stall, and no one appears to have built hot-swappable plate systems for web presses
Heat-set inks require paper to pass through long ovens at precise speeds, directly analogous to pipeline latency and inserted delays in GPU processing
High-speed cameras on press lines serve the same diagnostic role as GPU debug capture tools
money_quotes: If the only tool you have is an analogy to a hammer, every problem looks like a story about nails.
If Buzzfeed can make lists of things that nobody cares about, so can I.
The more you learn about these things, the more you realize how awesome they are, and you want to keep going.
It is literally just a list of ways that printing presses are like GPUs. There's no point to it whatsoever, except to try to impress upon the uninitiated just how cool modern printing presses are.
Remember back when texture changes were really expensive, and the GPU could only read a few textures at once? Well, modern printing presses are exactly like that.
If, by the time you reach the end of this list, you are motivated to watch a YouTube video on industrial web offset printing, my work here is done.
It's kind of ridiculous how accurate offset presses can be, especially when you consider that web presses are operating on paper at speeds of several feet per second.
key_insight: Modern industrial printing presses are remarkably analogous to GPUs in their architecture — from fixed-rate pipelines and multi-channel rendering to texture caches, pipeline stalls, and debug instrumentation — revealing that the fundamental engineering challenges of high-throughput image production transcend the digital/physical divide.
